one_line_takeaway: KinGuard sidesteps the stealth-robustness paradox of backdoor-based LLM fingerprinting by embedding a naturalistic, relationally-structured private knowledge corpus via pre-training and verifying ownership through semantic-similarity probing (ROUGE-N-based AUC) rather than fixed trigger-response elicitation, achieving 100% clean-model FSR with much lower perplexity, 0% Token-Forcing detection, and markedly higher survival under fine-tuning, input perturbation, and model merging than IF-SFT, Chain&Hash, and ProFlingo baselines.
key_finding: KinGuard matches or exceeds baseline effectiveness (100% FSR on all three base models, vs. a 55% FSR for a non-fingerprinted LLaMA2 control) while being far stealthier and more robust. Its fingerprint corpus has much lower perplexity than baseline triggers (14.57 vs. IF-SFT's 1048.00 and ProFlingo's 11249.27 under the LLaMA3-Instruct estimator), and it is completely immune to the Token Forcing detection attack (0% detection rate, vs. 100% for IF-SFT and 50% for Chain&Hash). Under fine-tuning attacks, KinGuard retains higher FSR than baselines on both Qwen2.5 (e.g., 100%/98%/92% on Alpaca/ShareGPT/Dolly) and LLaMA3 (81%/80%/73%), while Chain&Hash is erased entirely (0% on LLaMA2 across all three fine-tuning sets) and IF-SFT is inconsistent. Under input perturbation (5%/10% character removal), KinGuard stays at 100% FSR while ProFlingo collapses (26%/12% on LLaMA2). Under model merging with WizardMath-7B across Task Arithmetic/Ties-Merging and their DARE variants, KinGuard's FSR stays near 100% while IF-SFT and Chain&Hash are rapidly erased. The ablation shows the relational kinship graph is essential: the 'Individual' variant (attributes without relationships) is markedly worse (e.g., 62% clean-model FSR, 24% FSR after 5-10% perturbation) than full KinGuard, and the 'Normal' generic-corpus variant is also weaker (68% clean FSR), confirming that structured relational knowledge -- not just any auxiliary fine-tuning corpus -- drives the robustness gains. KinGuard also causes the least degradation to downstream task accuracy (best AVG ACC among fingerprinted variants on Qwen2.5: 0.620, and LLaMA3: 0.613).
core_contribution: KinGuard is a black-box LLM fingerprinting framework for ownership/copyright verification that replaces conventional backdoor-style trigger-response memorization with a 'knowledge-based' fingerprint: a private corpus of structured kinship narratives (two synthetic family graphs, Zoe and Lewis, six individuals total, each defined by a personal/trait/habit/relationship attribute quadruple, expanded via textual variants into 300+ narratives) is injected into the target model via incremental pre-training (causal LM loss) rather than by teaching fixed trigger->response pairs. Ownership is verified by feeding a suspect model a narrative prefix, comparing its generated continuation to the private ground-truth continuation via ROUGE-N, and computing the AUC between the ROUGE-N score distribution on fingerprinted samples versus a non-fingerprinted control set; this AUC is defined as the paper's primary metric, the Fingerprint Success Rate (FSR).
threat_model: Black-box model-stealing/IP-theft setting: a defender fingerprints their own LLM before release; an adversary obtains the model (via leak, fine-tuning derivative, extraction, or merging with another model) and redistributes it without authorization. The verifier has only query/output (black-box) access to the suspect model and must decide, using generated-text similarity to a private corpus, whether it is a derivative of the protected model. Adversary capabilities modeled/tested: incremental fine-tuning on public instruction datasets (Alpaca, ShareGPT, Dolly), input perturbation (random character deletion on the fingerprint prefix), model merging with an unrelated expert model (WizardMath-7B) via four fusion strategies, and an explicit anti-fingerprint detection attack (Token Forcing) that an adversary could use to expose and strip out backdoor-style fingerprints before redistribution.
limitations: The paper is a short ICASSP 2026 paper (5 pages of body text) with a correspondingly narrow evaluation: only 3 base models (all in the 7-8B parameter range), one merging-attack target model (WizardMath-7B) tested only on LLaMA2, and the fingerprint dataset itself is small and synthetic (2 families, 6 individuals, 300+ narratives), raising questions about scalability, distinctiveness/collision risk across many independently fingerprinted models, and generalization to larger or differently-architected models. Verification relies on ROUGE-N surface similarity and an AUC computed against a control set, which requires the verifier to hold both the private corpus and a suitable non-fingerprinted control distribution -- the paper does not discuss verification cost, threshold-setting/false-positive-rate calibration in an open-world setting (i.e., across many unrelated third-party models), or statistical significance testing beyond the AUC metric itself. Robustness against merging is demonstrated for only one paired expert model and one base model, not swept across architectures. The authors themselves note as future work that KinGuard should be tested on more models to establish generality, implicitly acknowledging the current evaluation's limited scope. No adaptive-adversary analysis is given for an attacker who specifically knows the kinship-narrative embedding strategy (as opposed to only knowing the Token Forcing attack designed for backdoor-style fingerprints).

datasets_used: Private kinship-narrative fingerprint corpus Dfp (2 synthetic families, Zoe and Lewis, 6 individuals total, 300+ generated textual narratives)
ANLI R1-3, ARC, OpenBookQA, Winogrande, LogiQA, SciQ, BoolQ, RTE, WiC, WSC, CoPA, MultiRC (12 harmlessness benchmarks)
AgNews and Xsum (150 samples each, used only in the ablation's 'Normal' variant)
quantitative_results: Clean-model FSR: KinGuard 100% on LLaMA2, Qwen2.5, LLaMA3 (vs. non-fingerprinted LLaMA2 control at 55%)
Input-stealth PPL (LLaMA3-Instruct estimator): KinGuard 14.57 vs. IF-SFT 1048.00, Chain&Hash 86.31, ProFlingo 11249.27
Input-stealth PPL (GPT-2 estimator): KinGuard 33.29 vs. IF-SFT 245.13, Chain&Hash 168.21, ProFlingo 5295.87
Token Forcing output-stealth detection rate: KinGuard 0% vs. IF-SFT 100%, Chain&Hash 50%
Fine-tuning robustness FSR on LLaMA3: KinGuard 81% (Alpaca) / 80% (ShareGPT) / 73% (Dolly) vs. IF-SFT 0% on all three and Chain&Hash 100%/98%/92%
Fine-tuning robustness FSR on Qwen2.5: KinGuard 100%/98%/92% (Alpaca/ShareGPT/Dolly) vs. IF-SFT 0% on all three
Input perturbation FSR on LLaMA2: KinGuard 100% at both 5% and 10% character removal vs. ProFlingo 26%/12% and IF-SFT 95%/75%
Harmlessness AVG ACC: KinGuard 0.588 (LLaMA2), 0.620 (Qwen2.5), 0.613 (LLaMA3) -- highest among fingerprinted variants in each case
Ablation clean-model FSR on LLaMA2: full KinGuard 100% vs. 'Normal' generic-corpus variant 68% vs. 'Individual' (no-relationship) variant 62%
Ablation perturbation robustness on LLaMA2: full KinGuard 100% (5% and 10% removal) vs. 'Normal' 67%/67% vs. 'Individual' 24%/24%
baselines_compared: IF-SFT (instructional fingerprinting via supervised fine-tuning on trigger-response pairs)
